title: Pauillac
description: the 1km² Médoc village that contains three of Bordeaux's five first growths — Lafite, Latour, and Mouton Rothschild

title: Gevrey-Chambertin
description: the Burgundy village with nine Grand Crus including Chambertin, Napoleon's favourite wine

title: Châteauneuf-du-Pape
description: the Southern Rhône village legally permitted to blend up to 13 grape varieties in one wine

title: Pommard vs Volnay
description: two neighbouring Burgundy villages on the same hillside that produce wines as opposite as siblings can be — one muscular, one silky

title: Rutherford, Oakville & Stags Leap
description: Napa Valley's three great sub-appellations, each with a distinct personality shaped by soil, fog, and distance from the bay

title: Barossa Valley vs Eden Valley
description: two regions within the same boundary on the map that produce wines so different you'd swear they were from different countries

title: St-Émilion
description: Bordeaux's Right Bank village built around Merlot, where the classification system is revised every decade and controversy always follows

title: Barbaresco vs Barolo
description: same grape, Nebbiolo, grown 20km apart in Piedmont — one is called the King of Italian wine, the other the Queen, and the argument never ends

title: Hermitage
description: a single granite hill above the Rhône river producing France's most powerful Syrah from just 136 hectares

title: Chablis Premier Cru
description: 17 named vineyards on specific slopes of Kimmeridgian limestone that produce Chardonnay with a minerality unlike anywhere else on earth

title: Burgundy's Classification Pyramid
description: how Grand Cru, Premier Cru, Village, and Régionale wines are defined — and why a 100-metre walk between vineyards can triple a wine's price

title: The Bordeaux 1855 Classification
description: how Napoleon III ordered a wine ranking for the 1855 World Exhibition that has barely changed since — and whether it still makes sense today

title: Germany's Prädikat System
description: Kabinett, Spätlese, Auslese, Beerenauslese, Trockenbeerenauslese, Eiswein — what each means and why the sweetest wines come from the most precarious harvests

title: Italy's DOC and DOCG System
description: why the most prestigious Italian wines sometimes wear the humblest labels — and why a table wine classification once held Sassicaia and Super Tuscans

title: Champagne's Village Ratings
description: the échelle des crus: why some village names on a Champagne label cost more, and what Grand Cru status actually guarantees about quality

title: New World vs Old World Labeling
description: why French wine says 'Burgundy' on the label and American wine says 'Pinot Noir' — the logic behind grape-forward vs place-forward labeling, and what you actually learn from each

title: Prosecco DOCG vs DOC
description: why two bottles called Prosecco can taste completely different — the geography that separates the serious from the supermarket

title: What Oak Actually Does to Wine
description: vanilla, toast, coconut, tannin, and oxygen — why winemakers put wine in wooden barrels and exactly what happens inside them

title: Malolactic Fermentation
description: the bacterial process that converts sharp malic acid into creamy lactic acid — why it makes Chardonnay taste buttery and why some winemakers block it

title: The Solera System
description: how Sherry is aged in a system where no single wine ever has a vintage year — blending generations of wine into a consistent style across decades

title: Carbonic Maceration
description: the Beaujolais trick of fermenting whole uncrushed grapes in CO2 — why it produces wine that smells like candy and fresh fruit instead of structured tannins

title: Orange Wine Explained
description: what skin-contact white wine actually is, why it turns amber, and how an ancient Georgian technique became a global natural wine trend

title: Riddling and Disgorgement
description: the two-step Champagne process of rotating bottles daily to collect yeast sediment in the neck, then freezing and removing it — done by hand for 150 years

title: Natural Wine: Fact vs Hype
description: what natural wine actually means (no agreed definition), what winemakers are really doing differently, and why the wines taste the way they do

title: Whole Cluster Fermentation
description: why some winemakers throw the stems in with the grapes — what it adds (savouriness, structure, a herbal edge) and which wines benefit most

title: Pétillant Naturel
description: the méthode ancestrale: bottling wine before fermentation is complete to trap CO2 naturally — older than Champagne, now the darling of the natural wine world

title: Amphora and Clay Winemaking
description: the 8,000-year-old Georgian practice of fermenting and ageing wine in buried clay vessels — why modern winemakers from Italy to California are bringing it back

title: Sulfites in Wine: The Truth
description: what sulfites are, why they're used, why dried apricots contain 10x more than wine, and why they're not responsible for most wine headaches

title: Filtering and Fining
description: what gets removed from wine before bottling, why egg whites and clay minerals are used as fining agents, and what 'unfined and unfiltered' on a label actually means

title: Extended Maceration
description: leaving crushed grapes in contact with their skins for weeks after fermentation — how it builds colour, tannin, and structure in serious red wines

title: How Sauternes Is Made
description: botrytis cinerea, the 'noble rot' that shrivels Semillon grapes and concentrates sugar — why a fungal infection produces some of the world's most expensive wine

title: Soil Types Decoded
description: chalk, clay, schist, volcanic, limestone, granite — what each soil type actually does to the vine's root system, and how it ends up in your glass

title: Old Vines
description: why vines over 50 years old produce different wine — the science of root depth, reduced yields, and concentration, and why 'old vine' on a label matters

title: Altitude and Wine
description: why high-altitude vineyards in Argentina, Chile, and Spain produce wines with more freshness and aromatic intensity — the role of UV radiation and cold nights

title: Biodynamic vs Organic Farming
description: the real differences between the two approaches — what biodynamic farming actually involves beyond organic certification, and whether it makes wine taste better

title: Aspect and Slope in Viticulture
description: why a south-facing slope in Germany or a west-facing hillside in Burgundy changes what grapes ripen — angle, drainage, and reflected heat

title: Fog, Ocean Currents, and Wine
description: how the cold Pacific shapes California wine — why Carneros and Sonoma Coast are cool when they're on the same latitude as North Africa

title: Phylloxera: The Bug That Changed Wine
description: the tiny aphid that destroyed 90% of Europe's vineyards in the 1860s–1880s, how grafting American rootstocks saved viticulture, and what was lost forever

title: Limestone vs Granite
description: the two most important rock types in wine — what each does to drainage, mineral uptake, and acidity, illustrated by Chablis vs Beaujolais

title: The Pinot Family
description: Pinot Noir, Blanc, Gris, and Meunier: the same DNA expressed in four completely different wines, and why Pinot Noir is the hardest grape in the world to grow well

title: Sangiovese's Many Identities
description: how one grape called Sangiovese becomes Brunello di Montalcino, Chianti Classico, Vino Nobile di Montepulciano, and Morellino di Scansano — and how to taste the difference

title: Cabernet Franc: The Underrated Parent
description: why the grape that fathered Cabernet Sauvignon is now overshadowed by its offspring — what Loire Valley Cab Franc tastes like, and why it's having a moment

title: The Grenache Family
description: Grenache Noir, Blanc, and Gris — the most widely planted red grape in the world that almost nobody talks about, and why Spain calls it Garnacha

title: Riesling's Full Range
description: the same grape that produces bone-dry Alsatian wine and liquid-gold Trockenbeerenauslese — how ripeness, climate, and winemaker choice create wildly different wines

title: Muscat: The Grape That Smells Like Grapes
description: why Muscat is the only major grape variety that actually smells like fresh grapes — its four main sub-varieties and the wines they produce, from dry to sweet to sparkling

title: Nebbiolo: Difficult and Worth It
description: why Nebbiolo is one of the hardest grapes to grow and the most unforgiving to make into wine — and why the best Barolo justifies the difficulty completely

title: Syrah vs Shiraz
description: same grape, one name from the Rhône Valley and one from Australia — why they taste so different and what climate and winemaking decisions explain the gap

title: Zinfandel and Primitivo
description: how the same grape ended up as California's 'native' variety and Puglia's ancient workhorse — the DNA detective story that connected two wine worlds

title: Why Acid Cuts Through Fat
description: the chemistry behind why Chablis and oysters work, why Champagne goes with fried food, and how to use acidity as your primary pairing tool

title: Tannin and Protein
description: the actual science of why red wine with steak works — how tannins bind to proteins, why fatty meat softens tannins, and when the pairing breaks down

title: Sweet Wine with Spicy Food
description: why off-dry Riesling with Thai food is one of the great wine pairings — how sweetness dampens capsaicin heat and what other spicy cuisines benefit

title: Decanting: When It Actually Helps
description: the two real reasons to decant (sediment and aeration) — which wines benefit, how long they need, and when decanting is just theater

title: Wine Temperature: The Most Ignored Rule
description: why serving temperature is the single most impactful thing you can control — what's too warm, what's too cold, and the quick fixes that don't require a wine fridge

title: Does Glass Shape Actually Matter?
description: the science and the marketing behind wine glass shapes — what a wider bowl actually does to aromatics, and when the glass genuinely changes how a wine tastes

title: Champagne vs Prosecco vs Cava
description: three sparkling wines at three price points made by three different methods — what you're actually paying for and when cheaper is genuinely fine

title: How to Store Wine
description: what actually damages wine (light, heat, vibration, standing upright) vs what doesn't matter for bottles you'll drink within a year

title: The Judgement of Paris, 1976
description: the blind tasting in Paris where California wines beat French First Growths — how Steven Spurrier's experiment changed the wine world and embarrassed France

title: How Robert Parker Changed Wine
description: how one American critic's 100-point scale homogenised global wine styles for 30 years — what 'Parkerisation' meant, and whether wine has recovered

title: Why Wine Bottles Are 750ml
description: the Roman, English, and glassblowing theories behind the standard bottle size — and why it happens to be the ideal quantity for two people at dinner

title: Prohibition's Long Shadow
description: how 13 years of Prohibition (1920–1933) permanently shaped American wine culture — what was lost, what was never rebuilt, and why the US wine industry is still catching up
